synthesis_text: Secure the WebSocket channel by upgrading to wss (TLS) and authenticating each handshake with a short‑lived signed JWT that carries a nonce/jti claim (to prevent token replay). Verify the JWT on every new connection and rotate signing keys regularly. Protect payload integrity by signing each message with an HMAC derived from a connection‑specific secret. For ordering and deduplication, attach a monotonic per‑message sequence number (or a server‑generated UUID event ID) to every event. The server stores the highest accepted sequence (or recent IDs) per client and discards any duplicate or out‑of‑order messages. On reconnect, the client reports the last sequence number it processed so the server can resume from the next event. Apply exponential backoff to client reconnect attempts to avoid connection storms and duplicate deliveries.

Secure the WebSocket with wss (TLS) and authenticate the upgrade using short‑lived signed JWTs; rotate signing keys and keep a revocation list to prevent token replay. On each reconnect the client includes the last received sequence number so the server can resume from the next event. Assign a strictly increasing sequence number (or server‑issued nonce) to every event; clients drop messages with lower or duplicate numbers and the server stores the highest accepted sequence per client to reject replays. Optionally buffer out‑of‑order messages or use application ACKs to confirm processing. Enable TCP keepalive and use exponential backoff with rate limits on reconnections to avoid storms and duplicate deliveries. Protect payload integrity by signing each message with an HMAC derived from a connection‑specific secret.

Secure the WebSocket channel by upgrading to wss (TLS) to protect confidentiality and integrity (N5:0:49e764c9) and authenticating each upgrade request with a short‑lived signed JWT or session cookie (N5:1:cc616271). Rotate signing keys regularly and maintain a revocation list to prevent replayed tokens (N1:4:09170734). Mitigate reconnection storms and token reuse by limiting reconnection rate, rotating the token on each new handshake, and applying exponential backoff (N5:6:467afb7c, N1:6:a3926ddb). Enforce ordering and deduplication by assigning strictly increasing sequence numbers per event and having clients discard messages with lower numbers (N1:0:28aaf70c); on reconnect, the client should send the last received sequence number so the server can resume from the next event (N8:2:b38a1b42). Maintain a short sliding window of recent message IDs per client to detect duplicates (N1:2:05276bff) and optionally store the highest accepted sequence in memory/DB. Sign each payload with an HMAC derived from a connection‑specific secret (e.g., from the auth token) and verify the signature on receipt (N5:4:47451c12). Include a timestamp with a narrow acceptance window (±30 s) to reject stale or replayed messages (N5:5:deb71c46).
